200	## Description (X.Y.Z)
201	```
202	
203	Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``
204	
205	### Pre-commit checklist
206	
207	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
208	
209	1. `cargo fmt`
210	2. `cargo clippy`
211	3. `cargo test`
212	4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
213	5. Retest after install
214	6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
215	7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
216	8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
217	   new subcommands, changed behavior)
218	
219	## Code Conventions
220	
221	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
222	
223	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
224	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
225	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
226	comment exists, not that it be long.
227	
228	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
229	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
230	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
231	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
232	non-obvious WHY.
233	
234	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
235	
236	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
237	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
238	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
239	
240	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
241	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
242	  self-evident.
243	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
244	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
245	
246	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
247	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
248	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
249	
250	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
251	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
252	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
253	
254	```rust
255	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
256	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
257	
258	match matches.len() {
259	    1 => {
260	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
261	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
262	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
263	    }
264	    // ...
265	}
266	```
267	
268	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
269	correct test behavior.
270	
271	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
272	
273	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
274	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
275	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
276	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
277	
278	### Recognize when stuck
279	
280	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
281	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
282	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
283	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
284	on X."
285	
286	## ochid Trailers
287	
288	Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
289	counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
290	path followed by the changeID:
291	
292	- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
293	- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`
294	
295	Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
296	is app repo, second is `.claude`).
297	
298	## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow
299	
300	**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
301	command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
302	preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
303	composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
304	prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
305	bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
306	local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
307	after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
308	recovery is forward-only.
309	
310	**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
311	Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
312	single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
313	is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
314	commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
315	finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
316	they happen rather than being batched until the end.
317	
318	### Run `vc-x1 push`
319	
320	```
321	vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
322	vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
323	vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
324	vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
325	vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
326	vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
327	vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
328	```
329	
330	The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:
331	
332	1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
333	   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
334	2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
335	   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
336	   reads right".
337	
338	Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
339	only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
340	pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.
341	
342	For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
343	and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.
344	
345	### Bot communication during the flow
346	
347	When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
348	each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
349	etc.):
350	
351	1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
352	   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
353	   something like:
354	
355	   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
356	   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.
357	
358	2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
359	   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.

## Description (X.Y.Z)

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

## ochid Trailers

Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
path followed by the changeID:

- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`

Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
they happen rather than being batched until the end.

### Run `vc-x1 push`

```
vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
```

The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:

1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
   reads right".

Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.

For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.

### Bot communication during the flow

When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
etc.):

1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
   something like:

   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.

2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
   Do **not** spell out the full
   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
   debugging).

3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
   detached and absolute-last; see "After finalize: stop and
   wait" below).

### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync` (still useful)

Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --no-dry-run` as its first step
so divergence is resolved before the build. Running sync manually
before you *start* editing is still cheap (one line when clean)
and surfaces remote changes earlier:

```
vc-x1 sync                            # dual-repo workspace
vc-x1 sync -R .                       # single-repo project
vc-x1 sync --quiet                    # silent; exit code signals result
```

Output shape:

- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all up-to-date`. Proceed.
- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`): per-repo fetch +
  state lines, then `dry-run — re-run with --no-dry-run to apply`.
  Inspect, then re-run with `--no-dry-run` (or resolve conflicts
  if rebase fails).
- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session per-step
or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit
files, run tools, or emit any text (prose, recaps, acknowledgements),
until the user explicitly directs you to continue. Treat finalize as
a hard stop for the whole turn. Any final words (e.g. "next is ...")
must be said in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the
finalize `Bash` call is the last thing in the turn and nothing
follows it.

This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a checkpoint
they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or take a break at.
Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and produces unwanted writes
between finalize and the next explicit instruction.

Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized at
this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.

### Late changes after push

If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes in, then re-push:

```
jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

(`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
writes so you rarely hit this case there.)

### Manual finalize fallback

If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no
tool calls, no additional output. If any work is done after
finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.
Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
already displays it.

Read the new CLAUDE.md. A few clarifying questions before I proceed:

1. **`--yes` and the two internal gates.** Push has two approval gates (review + message) surfaced by push itself, but the bot-communication section says to run push with `--yes --title --body` after in-conversation approval. 2. **Preflight overlap.** Push's preflight runs `sync + fmt + clippy + test` as its first step. Should I skip my own pre-commit checklist now (since push will run it and fail out if anything's broken), or still run `cargo fmt/clippy/test` ahead of time to catch issues before I present work for review? The former saves time; the latter avoids "work complete" followed by a preflight failure.

3. **Body content.** Old flow: app-repo body and session-repo body differed (app = code-change index mirroring chores; session = session-activity notes). Confirm?

content.text:
Source-code findings (all three answers grounded in `vc-x1/src/push.rs`):

- **Preflight (push.rs:784–803)**: runs `vc-x1 sync --no-dry-run`, `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo test`. Nothing else.
- **No `cargo install` / retest in preflight** (push.rs:780–781, explicit comment: *"pre-commit checklist (minus `cargo install` / retest, which are project-specific)"*). So step 4 stays on the bot.
- **Ochid trailer auto-appended** at the commit stages: `commit-app` appends `\n\nochid: /.claude/<claude_chid>` (push.rs:997); `commit-claude` appends `\n\nochid: /<app_chid>` (push.rs:1060). Template comment at push.rs:940: *"Body goes after a blank line. ochid trailers are appended"*. So the body passed via `--body` must **not** include an ochid trailer.
- **`--yes`** auto-approves review (push.rs:828–830) and message gates.

Both `actor-x1/CLAUDE.md` and `vc-template-x1/CLAUDE.md` are byte-identical — one diff applies to both.

**Proposed CLAUDE.md edits** (three, for agreement before applying):

**Edit A — `## Commit Message Style`** (around lines 112–125). Currently two bullets (`App-repo body` / `Session-repo body`) describe different bodies. Replace with three bullets describing the unified body + optional session-notes tail + ochid rule:

- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet = one file, one-line gist, mirroring `notes/chores-*.md`. Chores is the source of truth; the body is a scan-able index.
- **Session notes** (optional tail appended to the body): if the session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends, rationale — append a trailing subsection titled `## .claude session notes:` with those items as bullets. Same body (main content + any session-notes) lands in both commits.
- **Ochid trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you pass via `--body`. Push appends the correct per-repo trailer at commit-app (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>`) and commit-claude (`ochid: /<chid>`).

**Edit B — `### Pre-commit checklist`** (around lines 205–217). Keep the 8-step list as-is, but append a short clarifying paragraph:

> Push's preflight re-runs `vc-x1 sync` + `cargo fmt` + `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` + `cargo test` as a safety net. Run the checklist above locally anyway — fmt/clippy/test iteration is cheaper in-conversation than bouncing off preflight after review approval. Steps 4–5 (`cargo install --path .` and retest) are **not** in push's preflight (project-specific); the bot runs them.

**Edit C — `### Bot communication during the flow`** (around lines 338–340). Currently says:

> Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits; only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.

Minor trim to cross-reference Commit Message Style rather than duplicate the body rules:

> Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits; per-repo `ochid:` trailers are appended by push itself. See `## Commit Message Style` for body structure.

Agree on A / B / C? Any wording you'd tighten before I apply to both files?

## Commit Message Style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
  in-session work, not code changes.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

### Review before proposing the commit block

After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.

This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
review pause before its commit block appears.

Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
step before starting the next; don't ask.

### Notes references

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
   marker.

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

### Chores section headers

Chores section headers use trailing version format:

```
## Description (X.Y.Z)
```

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the

content.text:
Finding from the source: `push.rs:595-596` has `fn claude_path(root) -> root.join(".claude")` — **push hardcodes `.claude`**, while `fix_desc.rs`, `init.rs`, and friends actually read `[workspace].other-repo` from `.vc-config.toml` (and the template even emits `other-repo = ".."` in some modes, so the config is meant to vary). So CLAUDE.md is describing current reality for push, but the abstraction *is* real for the rest of the tool.

Three ways to clarify, ranked:

1. **Minimal (recommended)** — add a short "Terminology" block near the top defining `other-repo` as `[workspace].other-repo` from `.vc-config.toml` (default `.claude`, used as the concrete example throughout). Leave most existing `.claude` references alone, since they match what push actually does; only reword the few places where the label is conceptual (e.g. the session-notes subsection title could become `## <other-repo> session notes:` in description text, rendered literally as `## .claude session notes:` when composing for a `.claude`-default project).

2. **Full abstraction** — sweep `.claude` → `<other-repo>` throughout, add one "default is `.claude`" note. More correct-looking but misleading given push's hardcoding — readers may assume changing the config will Just Work.

3. **Fix push first, then (2)** — file a vc-x1 issue to teach push to read `other-repo` from config, then document the abstraction honestly. Bigger scope, probably right long-term.

The bot thinks (1) is the right call now: the abstraction exists for most of vc-x1 and is worth surfacing, but push's hardcoding means full abstraction in docs would overstate current behavior. Which flavor do you want — (1), (2), or (3)?

content.text:
Push halted at `push-app`. Current state:

**Done locally:**
- App commit created: `oxtpuuon 33d15293 chore: plan workspace for this repo (0.2.0-0)`
- `.claude` commit created: `xvwtkuyo c1639253` (same title)
- Both `main` bookmarks advanced to the new commits

**Blocked at `push-app`:**
```
error: Error: Non-tracking remote bookmark main@origin exists
Hint: Run `jj bookmark track main --remote=origin` to import the remote bookmark.
```

**Two issues to resolve, want your call on both:**

1. **Remote tracking.** The hint suggests `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .`. Safe and reversible — just teaches jj to track the existing remote bookmark. After that, `vc-x1 push main --yes --from push-app` resumes the flow.

2. **`.vc-x1/push-state.toml` leaked into the app commit.** Push's own warning at the top said so: *"state dir '.vc-x1' is not in ... `.gitignore` — add a '/.vc-x1' line so in-progress push-state files don't get committed."* The review diff confirms: `.vc-x1/push-state.toml | 6 +` is part of commit `33d15293`. Options:

   - **(a) Fix forward**: add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore` as its own subsequent commit, push `33d15293` as-is. Simplest. Cost: one stale push-state file in history.
   - **(b) Rewrite the commit**: while it's still unpushed, `jj edit @-`, remove the file from the commit (via `jj file untrack` + delete from working copy), add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore` in the same commit, pop back with `jj new`. Clean history. `.claude` commit is untouched. More moving parts but nothing destructive if I'm careful.
   - **(c) Abandon and redo**: abandon both commits, fix `.gitignore` first, start push over. Cleanest reset, but we lose the `.claude` commit which was fine.

The bot thinks **(b)** is right — the commit isn't pushed yet so we can still rewrite it safely, and (a) would permanently bake a spurious state file into history. But (b) needs your approval before I touch the mutable commit, and if (b) goes wrong we fall back to (a).

Your call: (a) / (b) / (c), and go-ahead on the `jj bookmark track` step?

content.text:
Agreed on the approach. Analysis:

**Key insight:** the user's plan doesn't require removing `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` from the existing commit — it only requires `.gitignore` to exclude future changes. The file stays tracked in commit `33d15293` as a one-time scar, but once gitignored, subsequent modifications to it (as push advances through stages) won't leak into future commits. That keeps `push-state.toml` alive on disk for the resume, and keeps the amendment minimal.

**Proposed actions (in order):**

1. **Add `/.vc-x1` to `.gitignore`** (actor-x1 only, not vc-template-x1).

2. **Add a gitignore note to CLAUDE.md.** Best spot: a short "Prerequisites" paragraph near the top of `## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow`, before `### Run vc-x1 push`:

   > **Prerequisite.** `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's
   > in-progress state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get
   > committed. Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
   > This only needs to be set up once per repo.

3. **Squash both changes into `@-`** (commit `33d15293`) via `jj squash -R .`. Since `/.vc-x1` is in `.gitignore` at snapshot time, the `push-state.toml` modifications push made during the failed run won't appear as tracked changes in `@`, so the squash carries only the two intended edits (`.gitignore` + `CLAUDE.md`). The already-tracked `push-state.toml` inside `33d15293` remains — scar accepted.

4. **Track the remote bookmark**: `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .`. Reversible, just teaches jj to track `main@origin`.

5. **Re-run push**: `vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."`. Push reads `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` (stage = `push-app`), resumes there, runs `push-app` → `finalize-claude`. Commits + bookmarks from the failed run stand.

**Risks I want to flag:**

- If `jj squash` somehow picks up push-state.toml changes in `@` (e.g. if push wrote to it after `.gitignore` was updated), those would end up in the squashed commit. The bot thinks this won't happen because push hasn't run since the original failure — but let's verify by inspecting `jj st` after the `.gitignore` + `CLAUDE.md` edits, before the squash.

- If the resumed `push-app` still fails (e.g. the `jj bookmark track` didn't fully resolve the issue), we're in the same spot as now — recoverable, just iterate.

- Push's preflight runs again on resume. If the squash produced any state that makes `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test` unhappy, it'll fail at preflight. These are doc-only edits though, so expected clean.

OK to proceed?

content.text:
Two findings from the source:

**Finding 1 — the check exists, just in the wrong place.** `finalize.rs:158–229` has `find_non_tracking_remote` with the exact error message format we just hit (`"bookmark '<b>' has non-tracking remote '<b>@<remote>' — run jj bookmark track ..."`). So finalize pre-flights this. **Push's preflight does not** — `push.rs:784–803` only runs `sync` + `fmt` + `clippy` + `test`. That's inconsistent: push fails with a raw jj error at stage `push-app`, while finalize would refuse earlier with a clean message. The bot thinks moving (or duplicating) the check into push's preflight is a legit vc-x1 improvement worth filing.

**Finding 2 — no code path in sync or push untracks a bookmark.** I scanned both: no `jj bookmark forget`, no `jj bookmark untrack`, no `--no-track` anywhere. `sync` does `jj git fetch` + `jj bookmark set <b> -r <b>@<remote>` (fast-forward) / `jj rebase` — never `untrack` or `forget`. Push calls `jj bookmark set` and `jj git push --bookmark` — also never untracks.

**The bot thinks** this is the common jj behavior, not tool-caused: `jj git fetch` creates remote bookmarks as **non-tracking by default**. They only become tracked once someone runs `jj bookmark track <b>@<remote>` explicitly, and that state is then persisted. If this repo was initialized/cloned in a way that never called `track`, then all fetches since produce a non-tracking `main@origin` that any push will trip on. Sync doesn't untrack — it just never tracks in the first place if tracking wasn't set up.

**Upshot for our current decision:**
- `jj bookmark track main@origin -R .` is safe, correct, and one-time (won't need to re-run on future pushes).
- Worth filing a vc-x1 issue to copy the finalize check into push's preflight — catching this at preflight (before commits happen) is cleaner than catching it at push-app (after commits are locked in locally). This would have saved us from landing in this half-applied state right now.
- Also worth asking: should `vc-x1 init` (or `vc-x1 sync` on first run) auto-track? Probably yes — same reasoning. But out of scope for the current fix.

Still want me to proceed with the plan I outlined (gitignore + CLAUDE.md note → squash → `jj bookmark track` → resume push)?

local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;

## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Prerequisite — one-time per repo:**

- `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's in-progress
  state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get committed.
  Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
- The target bookmark must have a tracking remote
  (`jj bookmark track <bookmark>@origin -R .`). `jj git fetch`
  creates remote bookmarks as non-tracking by default; push
  currently surfaces this as a raw jj error at `push-app` after
  commits are locally made, so confirm tracking is set up
  before the first push in a new clone.

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.**
